PROJECT APPRAISAL DOCUMENT

ON A

PROPOSED CREDIT

IN THE AMOUNT OF

USD 50 MILLION

TO THE

REPUBLIC OF TESTLAND

FOR THE

FRAGILITY-SENSITIVE COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT PROJECT

June 15, 2025

Social Protection and Jobs Global Practice
Africa Region

This document has a restricted distribution and may be used by recipients only in the performance of their
official duties. Its contents may not otherwise be disclosed without World Bank authorization.

January 1 - December 31

WORLD BANK STANDARD DISCLAIMERS

Vice President:            Jane A. Smith, AFRVP
Country Director:          Robert B. Jones, AFCTL
Practice Manager:          Sarah C. Williams, GSP07
Task Team Leader(s):       Michael D. Brown, GSP07
                           Amina E. Hassan, GSURR

================================================================================

I. STRATEGIC CONTEXT

A. Country Context

1. Testland is a low-income country in Sub-Saharan Africa with a population of approximately 22 million.
The country has experienced a protracted period of fragility following a civil conflict that ended in 2015.
The Regional Risk Assessment for Testland (2024) identified rising inter-communal tensions in the northern
and eastern provinces as a primary driver of fragility. The assessment specifically highlights that fragility
in border districts has increased due to spillover effects from neighboring countries.

2. According to the Regional Risk Assessment, the country scores in the bottom quartile on institutional
legitimacy indicators, reflecting decades of governance deficits and state retrenchment from service
delivery in rural areas. The fragility trap is self-reinforcing: weak state institutions fail to deliver
services, reducing trust in government, which in turn weakens social cohesion.

3. Macro-fiscal indicators have improved since the peace agreement, with GDP growth averaging 4.2 percent
annually over the 2018-2024 period. However, distributional outcomes remain highly unequal, with poverty
concentrated in FCV-affected districts.

B. Sectoral and Institutional Context

4. The social protection sector in Testland is fragmented, with multiple programmes operating in parallel
under different ministries. The Ministry of Social Affairs (MoSA) has nominal coordination responsibility
but lacks the political authority to integrate cross-ministerial programmes.

5. The most recent fragility assessment (2023) found that existing social protection targeting mechanisms
inadvertently excluded conflict-affected IDPs, reinforcing perceptions of state bias among displaced
communities. This institutional context creates both risk and opportunity for the proposed operation.

6. The World Bank has supported social protection in Testland since 2010 through a series of projects.
Lessons learned from the portfolio include: (i) cash transfers are effective in FCV settings when linked
to community-driven targeting; (ii) grievance mechanisms must be accessible to marginalized groups;
(iii) FCV-sensitive M&E systems are critical for adaptive management.

C. Higher Level Objectives

7. The project directly supports the Country Partnership Framework (CPF) for Testland FY2023-2028, which
identifies social resilience and inclusion as a core pillar. The CPF explicitly acknowledges the FCV
context and calls for conflict-sensitive project design.

8. The project also contributes to the WBG FCV Strategy 2020-2025 objective of helping countries
transition out of fragility (Pillar 3), with secondary contributions to preventing conflict (Pillar 1)
through social cohesion strengthening components.

================================================================================

II. PROJECT DEVELOPMENT OBJECTIVES

A. PDO

9. The Project Development Objective is to increase access to social services for conflict-affected
communities in targeted districts, and to strengthen the capacity of local institutions to deliver
services in a conflict-sensitive manner.

10. Key results indicators include:
    (a) Number of direct project beneficiaries receiving cash transfers (disaggregated by gender, IDP status)
    (b) Percentage of beneficiaries reporting satisfaction with targeting fairness
    (c) Number of functional GRM cases resolved within 30 days
    (d) Percentage of project districts with updated conflict sensitivity assessments

B. Project Beneficiaries

11. The project will directly benefit approximately 250,000 households (estimated 1.25 million individuals)
in the five most FCV-affected districts. Of these, at least 40 percent are internally displaced persons
(IDPs) or returnee households.

12. Indirect beneficiaries include district-level government staff who will receive capacity building, and
community structures that will be strengthened through the participatory implementation approach.

================================================================================

III. PROJECT DESCRIPTION

A. Project Components

13. Component 1: Conflict-Sensitive Cash Transfers (USD 32 million). This component will finance cash
transfers to eligible households in targeted FCV districts. Targeting will use a community-based process
with built-in conflict sensitivity safeguards to avoid elite capture or exclusion of marginalized groups.

14. Component 2: Local Institutional Capacity Building (USD 12 million). This component will finance
training and technical assistance to district-level government institutions, with a focus on conflict-
sensitive service delivery, GRM establishment, and FCV-informed planning.

15. Component 3: Adaptive Management and FCV Monitoring (USD 4 million). This component will establish
a real-time monitoring system including a Grievance Redress Mechanism, conflict sensitivity tracking, and
regular FCV situation assessments to allow for project adaptation.

16. Component 4: Contingency Emergency Response Component — CERC (USD 2 million). Provides a
pre-positioned mechanism to rapidly reallocate project resources in response to conflict escalation
or humanitarian crises during the project period.

================================================================================

IV. IMPLEMENTATION

A. Institutional and Implementation Arrangements

17. The project will be implemented by a dedicated Project Implementation Unit (PIU) housed within the
Ministry of Social Affairs. The PIU will be staffed by a full-time FCV specialist, a social inclusion
officer, and a dedicated GRM coordinator.

18. Implementation will be carried out at the district level through District Social Development Committees
(DSDCs), which include community representatives, local government officials, and civil society
organizations. DSDCs are required to include at least 30 percent women and at least one representative
from IDP communities.

19. Financial management arrangements follow OP/BP 10.00 on Investment Project Financing. The designated
account will be maintained at the Central Bank of Testland. Quarterly financial reports will be submitted
to the World Bank within 45 days of the end of each quarter.

B. Results Monitoring and Evaluation

20. The results monitoring system will include quarterly progress reports, annual project assessments, and
a dedicated FCV conflict tracking module. Baselines have been established for all PDO indicators through
a household survey conducted in March 2025.

21. A midterm review will be conducted 30 months after effectiveness. An independent impact evaluation
is planned using a difference-in-differences design, comparing outcomes in project districts against
matched comparison districts.

================================================================================

V. KEY RISKS

SYSTEMATIC OPERATIONS RISK-RATING TOOL (SORT)

Risk Category                                      Rating
---------------------------------------------------------
Political and Governance                           High
Macroeconomic                                      Moderate
Sector Strategies and Policies                     Moderate
Technical Design of Project or Program             Moderate
Institutional Capacity for Implementation          Substantial
Fiduciary                                          Moderate
Environment and Social                             Substantial
Stakeholders                                       High
Other (Security / Conflict Escalation)             High
OVERALL                                            High

22. Political and Governance Risk (High): The fragile political environment in Testland, combined with
upcoming elections, creates significant risk of political interference in project targeting. Mitigation
includes community-based targeting with independent verification.

23. Security/Conflict Escalation Risk (High): Active conflict in two of the five target districts could
disrupt implementation and endanger project staff and beneficiaries. Mitigation includes a CERC component,
real-time security monitoring, and a conflict-contingent implementation protocol.

24. Institutional Capacity Risk (Substantial): District-level government capacity is limited. Mitigation
includes the capacity building component and embedding of FCV specialists in the PIU.

================================================================================

VI. APPRAISAL SUMMARY

A. Economic and Financial Analysis

25. The economic analysis is based on a cost-benefit framework. The net present value (NPV) of the
project is estimated at USD 78.4 million under the base scenario, with an economic rate of return of
17.3 percent. Sensitivity analysis shows the project remains economically viable even under scenarios
with 30 percent cost overruns or 20 percent shortfalls in benefit realization.

26. The fiscal impact on the government of Testland is assessed as manageable over the project period.

B. Technical

27. The technical design of the cash transfer mechanism is based on best practice from comparable FCV
contexts. The payment mechanism (mobile money) is appropriate given telecommunications infrastructure
in target districts.

C. Financial Management

28. Financial management arrangements comply with OP/BP 10.02 on Financial Management. An FM assessment
was conducted in April 2025 and found the PIU's FM capacity to be adequate with minor gaps. Agreed
actions include: (i) recruitment of a qualified FM specialist before effectiveness; (ii) installation of
accounting software; (iii) establishment of internal audit function within 6 months of effectiveness.

29. Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers apply to all procurement under this project. A Project
Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) has been prepared and approved.

D. Procurement

30. Procurement will be carried out in accordance with the Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers
(July 2016, revised November 2020). The PPSD identifies the following major procurement packages:
consultancy services for targeting, IT systems for M&E, and training delivery contracts.

31. OP/BP 10.02 does not apply to procurement activities; all procurement follows the Procurement
Regulations framework referenced above.

E. Social (including Safeguards)

32. A Social Assessment was conducted to identify potential adverse social impacts. Key findings include:
(i) significant risk of elite capture in targeting without adequate community oversight; (ii) GBV risks
linked to cash transfers in households with male-dominated financial control; (iii) social tension risks
from perceptions of biased targeting.

33. A Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) has been designed with multiple access channels including
community liaisons, SMS reporting, and district-level complaint desks. The GRM is accessible to persons
with disabilities and those with limited literacy. The GRM design incorporates lessons from similar
FCV contexts where anonymous reporting improved uptake among conflict-affected populations.

34. A Stakeholder Engagement Plan has been prepared (see Annex 7) and outlines how affected communities
will be engaged throughout project implementation.

F. Environment (including Safeguards)

35. The project is classified as Environmental Risk Category B. No significant adverse environmental
impacts are expected. An Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) has been prepared,
reviewed, and disclosed.

36. Climate co-benefits are positive: strengthened community resilience reduces vulnerability to
climate-induced displacement, which can be a conflict trigger in the Testland context.

G. World Bank Grievance Redress

37. Communities and individuals who believe that they are adversely affected by a World Bank supported
project may submit complaints to existing project-level grievance redress mechanisms or the World Bank's
Grievance Redress Service (GRS). The GRS ensures that complaints received are promptly reviewed in order
to address project-related concerns. Project affected communities and individuals may submit their
complaint to the World Bank's independent Inspection Panel which determines whether harm occurred, or
could occur, as a result of World Bank non-compliance with its policies and procedures.

================================================================================

Annex 1: Results Framework and Monitoring

PDO Indicators

Indicator 1: Number of direct project beneficiaries receiving cash transfers
    Baseline: 0 | Year 1: 100,000 | Year 2: 200,000 | Year 3 (End): 250,000
    Disaggregation: Gender (M/F), IDP status (IDP/Host/Returnee)

Indicator 2: Percentage of beneficiaries reporting targeting fairness
    Baseline: N/A | Year 1: — | Year 2: 65% | Year 3 (End): 75%

Indicator 3: GRM cases resolved within 30 days
    Baseline: 0 | Year 1: 50 | Year 2: 75 | Year 3 (End): 85

Do-No-Harm Indicators

The following Do-No-Harm indicators will be tracked quarterly to ensure the project does not exacerbate
conflict dynamics:

    - Incidents of targeting-related social tension (by district)
    - Number of beneficiaries from previously excluded groups (IDPs, ethnic minorities)
    - GRM case types disaggregated by conflict-related grievances
    - Conflict sensitivity assessment scores (annual, per district)

Intermediate Results Indicators

Indicator 4: Number of district staff trained in conflict-sensitive service delivery
    Baseline: 0 | Year 1: 120 | Year 2: 240 | Year 3 (End): 300

Indicator 5: Number of functional DSDCs with IDP representation meeting quarterly
    Baseline: 0 | Year 1: 5 | Year 2: 5 | Year 3 (End): 5

================================================================================

Annex 2: Detailed Project Description

This annex provides additional technical detail on project component design.

2.1 Component 1 Detail — Cash Transfer Mechanism

The cash transfer amount (USD 25 per household per month) was determined based on a consumption gap
analysis. The payment cycle is monthly; payments will be made via mobile money operators active in
target districts.

2.2 Component 2 Detail — Capacity Building Curriculum

The capacity building curriculum covers: conflict analysis for non-specialists, GRM establishment and
management, FCV-sensitive targeting procedures, and adaptive management tools.

2.3 Component 3 Detail — Monitoring System Architecture

The monitoring system uses ODK-based data collection tools, with real-time dashboards accessible to
the PIU and the World Bank task team. Data security protocols are in place.

================================================================================

Annex 3: Implementation Arrangements

Project Governance Structure

The project will be governed by a Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by the Minister of Social
Affairs and including representatives from the Ministry of Finance, Ministry of Interior, and civil
society. The PSC will meet semi-annually.

Staffing Plan

The PIU will be staffed with the following key positions:
    - Project Coordinator (1 FTE)
    - FCV Specialist (1 FTE) — responsible for conflict sensitivity mainstreaming, quarterly FCV
      situation assessments, and liaison with security monitoring systems
    - Social Inclusion Officer (1 FTE)
    - GRM Coordinator (1 FTE)
    - Financial Management Specialist (1 FTE)
    - Procurement Specialist (1 FTE)

Role of the FCV Specialist

The FCV Specialist will prepare quarterly conflict sensitivity reports, serve as the primary liaison
with the Regional Risk Assessment update process, and advise the PIU on adaptive management decisions
triggered by the CERC component.

================================================================================

Annex 4: Implementation Support Plan

The World Bank task team will provide implementation support as follows:

    - Two implementation support missions per year (on-site + remote as security permits)
    - Continuous FM/procurement monitoring via interim unaudited financial reports
    - Annual field visits by the TTL and FCV specialist

Supervision budget is estimated at USD 250,000 per year for the 3-year project period.

================================================================================

Annex 5: Economic Analysis

This annex provides the full details of the economic and financial analysis summarized in Section VI.A.

5.1 NPV Calculations

NPV calculations are based on a 12 percent social discount rate and a 10-year benefit horizon (including
post-project sustainability of capacity investments). The base case NPV of USD 78.4 million is driven
primarily by reduced household vulnerability (valued using consumption gains) and reduced conflict costs
(valued using UNODC conflict cost estimates for comparable contexts).

Sensitivity tests:
    - Scenario A (+30% costs): NPV = USD 55.2 million (ERR = 12.1%)
    - Scenario B (-20% benefits): NPV = USD 48.7 million (ERR = 10.8%)
    - Scenario C (conflict escalation — 50% implementation disruption): NPV = USD 22.1 million (ERR = 7.3%)

5.2 Distributional Analysis

The distributional analysis confirms that 78 percent of benefits accrue to the bottom two income quintiles.
IDP households receive disproportionately higher benefits per capita given their elevated vulnerability.

================================================================================

Annex 6: Financial Analysis

6.1 Financial Sustainability

Post-project financial sustainability depends on government budget allocations for the cash transfer
programme. The Ministry of Finance has provided a fiscal commitment letter confirming plans to
progressively absorb programme costs from Year 2 onward.

6.2 Financial Controls Assessment

Standard World Bank fiduciary procedures apply. OP/BP 10.02 compliance has been assessed. The PIU will
maintain a separate designated account. External audits will be conducted annually.

================================================================================

Annex 7: Social (including Safeguards)

Stakeholder Engagement Plan

A comprehensive Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) was prepared in March 2025 and publicly disclosed.
The SEP identifies the following stakeholder groups and engagement approaches:

    - Beneficiary communities: community information sessions, participatory targeting workshops
    - IDPs and returnees: dedicated outreach through IDP community liaisons
    - Local government: regular coordination meetings with DSDCs
    - Civil society organizations: quarterly coordination forum
    - Vulnerable groups (women, persons with disabilities, ethnic minorities): tailored engagement
      modalities including women-only focus groups and accessible information formats

The SEP also includes a time-bound consultation plan for project restructuring or component changes.

Gender-Based Violence (GBV) Risk Mitigation

The Social Assessment identified elevated GBV risks associated with cash transfers. Mitigation measures
include: (i) joint enrollment of household heads and female co-signatories for payment; (ii) GBV
referral pathways embedded in the GRM; (iii) GBV risk monitoring integrated into the conflict
sensitivity tracking module.

================================================================================

BORROWER COMMENTS

The Government of Testland reviewed the Project Appraisal Document and confirms its agreement with
the project design, safeguards arrangements, and financial management requirements. The government
notes that the cash transfer amount should be reviewed for adequacy at the midterm review given
potential inflation impacts.

Signed: Minister of Social Affairs, Testland
Date: June 10, 2025

================================================================================

[END OF DOCUMENT]
